section.title: Save Me, O God
section.sectionRationale: One of the great passion psalms of the Old Testament, quoted more often in the New Testament than almost any other psalm, this lament moves from David sinking in miry, overwhelming waters through undeserved reproach for God's sake, gall and vinegar offered for comfort, a sustained plea that God's justice fall on unrepentant enemies, and finally to a vow of praise and hope for Zion's restoration, its details fulfilled directly in Christ's zeal, thirst, and abandonment at the cross.
section.thesis: David's cry from waters up to his neck, borne out of undeserved reproach endured for God's own sake, is taken up by the New Testament as the very voice of Christ, whose zeal for His Father's house, whose thirst answered with vinegar, and whose betrayer's judgment are each traced to this psalm, showing that even its hardest verses, the plea for God's justice on the unrepentant, are honest, Spirit-given prayer entrusting vengeance to God rather than seizing it for oneself.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: the cry from overwhelming waters and miry depths, verses 1-6; undeserved reproach endured for God's sake, becoming a stranger even to family, verses 7-12; a renewed plea for rescue from the mire, verses 13-18; insults, gall, and vinegar as the psalm's climax of undeserved suffering, verses 19-21; the sustained imprecation praying God's justice on the unrepentant, verses 22-28; a vow of praise that pleases God more than sacrifice, verses 29-33; and the closing hope that heaven, earth, and Zion's rebuilt cities would praise Him, verses 34-36.

units.label: Save me, O God
units.note: the cry from overwhelming waters and miry depths

verses.text: Save me, O God, for the waters are up to my neck.
I have sunk into the miry depths, where there is no footing; I have drifted into deep waters, where the flood engulfs me.
I am weary from my crying; my throat is parched. My eyes fail, looking for my God.
Those who hate me without cause outnumber the hairs of my head; many are those who would destroy me, my enemies for no reason. Though I did not steal, I must repay.
You know my folly, O God, and my guilt is not hidden from You.
May those who hope in You not be ashamed through me, O Lord GOD of Hosts; may those who seek You not be dishonored through me, O God of Israel.

units.label: Zeal for Your house has consumed me
units.note: undeserved reproach, a stranger even to family

verses.text: For I have endured scorn for Your sake, and shame has covered my face.
I have become a stranger to my brothers and a foreigner to my mother's sons,
because {{qinah|zeal}} for Your house has consumed me, and the insults of those who insult You have fallen on me.
I wept and fasted, but it brought me reproach.
I made sackcloth my clothing, and I was sport to them.
Those who sit at the gate mock me, and I am the song of drunkards.
But my prayer to You, O Lord, is for a time of favor. In Your abundant loving devotion, O God, answer me with Your sure salvation.
Rescue me from the mire and do not let me sink; deliver me from my foes and out of the deep waters.
Do not let the floods engulf me or the depths swallow me up; let not the Pit close its mouth over me.
Answer me, O Lord, for Your loving devotion is good; turn to me in keeping with Your great compassion.
Hide not Your face from Your servant, for I am in distress. Answer me quickly!
Draw near to my soul and redeem me; ransom me because of my foes.

units.label: They gave me gall and vinegar

verses.text: You know my reproach, my shame and disgrace. All my adversaries are before You.
Insults have broken my heart, and I am in despair. I looked for sympathy, but there was none, for comforters, but I found no one.
They poisoned my food with {{rosh|gall}} and gave me vinegar to quench my thirst.

units.label: Pour out Your wrath upon them
units.note: the sustained plea for God's justice on the unrepentant

verses.text: May their table become a snare; may it be a retribution and a trap.
May their eyes be darkened so they cannot see, and their backs be bent forever.
Pour out Your wrath upon them, and let Your burning anger overtake them.
May their place be deserted; let there be no one to dwell in their tents.
For they persecute the one You struck and recount the pain of those You wounded.
Add iniquity to their iniquity; let them not share in Your righteousness.
May they be blotted out of the Book of Life and not listed with the righteous.

units.label: I will praise God's name in song
units.note: a vow of praise that pleases God more than sacrifice

verses.text: But I am in pain and distress; let Your salvation protect me, O God.
I will praise God's name in song and exalt Him with thanksgiving.
And this will please the Lord more than an ox, more than a bull with horns and hooves.
The humble will see and rejoice. You who seek God, let your hearts be revived!
For the Lord listens to the needy and does not despise His captive people.

units.label: Let heaven and earth praise Him
units.note: the closing hope for Zion's restoration

verses.text: Let heaven and earth praise Him, the seas and everything that moves in them.
For God will save Zion and rebuild the cities of Judah, that they may dwell there and possess it.
The descendants of His servants will inherit it, and those who love His name will settle in it.

qinah.gloss: Zeal, jealous devotion, an intense, consuming passion for God's honor. John's Gospel applies this very word directly to Jesus when He cleansed the temple, John 2:17, seeing His action as the fulfillment of this psalm's zeal that 'consumed' the psalmist.

rosh.gloss: Gall, a bitter, poisonous herb, mixed here with food as an act of cruelty toward the sufferer. Together with the vinegar offered for thirst, this detail is fulfilled precisely at the cross, where Jesus was offered wine mixed with gall and, later, sour wine, Matthew 27:34, 48.

basic.context: David cries out from what feels like drowning, waters up to his neck, hated without cause, and estranged even from his own family because of his zeal for God's house. He is mocked, given gall and vinegar instead of comfort, and prays urgently both for his own rescue and for God's justice on his unrepentant enemies.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm is quoted more often in the New Testament than almost any other, applied directly to Christ's zeal, His thirst on the cross, and the judgment on His betrayer. Its imprecations are not personal vengeance but a Spirit-inspired prayer entrusting justice to God alone.
basic.originalLanguages: David's qinah, zeal, for God's house consumes him, verse 9, the very word John applies to Jesus cleansing the temple, John 2:17. And his enemies give him rosh, gall, mixed with his food, verse 21, fulfilled precisely at the cross, Matthew 27:34.
basic.typology: Nearly every major section of this psalm is applied to Christ in the New Testament, His zeal for the temple, John 2:17, being hated without cause, John 15:25, His thirst answered with gall and vinegar, Matthew 27:34, 48, and the imprecation against His betrayer's household, applied to Judas in Acts 1:20.
basic.christ: This is one of the clearest messianic psalms in the Psalter. Jesus' disciples remembered 'zeal for Your house will consume Me' when He cleansed the temple, John 2:17. Jesus quoted verse 4 of Himself, 'they hated Me without a cause,' John 15:25. He fulfilled verse 21 literally on the cross, given sour wine, John 19:28-30. And Peter applies the imprecation of verse 25 directly to Judas Iscariot's fate, Acts 1:20.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who hears the cry of the drowning and the falsely accused, who allows His anointed to suffer reproach for His own sake, and whose coming justice on the unrepentant, prayed for honestly here, is neither denied nor apologized for anywhere in Scripture, but is entrusted entirely to Him rather than seized by human hands.
basic.crossReferences: John 2:17 applies this psalm's zeal for God's house directly to Jesus cleansing the temple. Matthew 27:34, 48 fulfill the gall and vinegar offered to Jesus on the cross. Romans 11:9-10 and Acts 1:20 apply this psalm's imprecations to the judgment on unbelieving Israel and on Judas respectively.
basic.oneStory: David's undeserved suffering for God's sake, his thirst answered with gall and vinegar, and his plea that God's justice fall on the unrepentant, all find their fulfillment in Christ, whose zeal, thirst, and betrayal this psalm precisely anticipates, and whose cross absorbs the very reproach David describes so that all who trust Him might finally, fully be saved.

context.literary: A lengthy individual lament moving from the crisis of overwhelming waters, verses 1-6, through undeserved reproach for God's sake, verses 7-12, renewed plea for rescue, verses 13-18, the climax of gall and vinegar, verses 19-21, sustained imprecation, verses 22-28, a vow of praise, verses 29-33, and closing hope for Zion, verses 34-36.
context.historical: Composed by David during a period of intense, undeserved persecution connected to his devotion to God's house and worship, its precise historical occasion is not narrated, but its themes of false accusation, family estrangement, and public mockery fit patterns of opposition David faced repeatedly.
context.canonical: Along with Psalm 22, this is one of the two most extensively quoted psalms in the New Testament's account of Christ's passion, its details of zeal, hatred without cause, gall and vinegar, and a betrayer's judgment each explicitly cited as fulfilled in Jesus.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring an experience of undeserved, God-related suffering honestly before the Lord, pleading both for personal rescue and for God's just judgment on unrepentant persecutors, while trusting that judgment entirely to God rather than executing it personally.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's own specific experience of suffering for God's sake; it prescribes as an abiding pattern honest lament that includes both urgent personal plea and confident appeal to God's justice, entrusted to Him alone rather than taken into human hands.

debates.question: How should Christians read the imprecations of verses 22-28, praying that enemies be blotted out of the Book of Life, in light of Jesus' command to love enemies and pray for those who persecute us?

views.label: The imprecations are sober, prophetic words of judgment spoken by God's inspired, anointed spokesman concerning the specific, unrepentant enemies of God's righteous purposes, not personal vindictiveness, and the New Testament itself quotes them approvingly, Romans 11:9-10, Acts 1:20.
views.case: Paul quotes this very passage in Romans 11 to explain God's righteous judgment on unbelieving Israel, and Peter applies verse 25 to Judas in Acts 1:20, showing the New Testament treats these words as reliable, Spirit-inspired declarations of coming judgment rather than sinful vengeance to be corrected or apologized for.

views.label: The imprecations function primarily as an honest expression of righteous anger at real injustice, entrusted fully to God's judgment rather than acted upon, modeling for believers today how to hand over desire for retribution to God rather than either suppressing it or acting on it themselves.
views.case: The psalm never calls on David or anyone else to personally harm these enemies; the entire section is addressed to God as prayer, consistent with Paul's later instruction, 'never avenge yourselves, but leave room for the wrath of God,' Romans 12:19, which itself quotes this same psalm's imagery.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree these words are not sinful personal revenge but are addressed to God alone, trusting Him to bring perfect justice; Jesus' command to love enemies does not erase the reality of final judgment but calls believers, following Christ's own pattern on the cross, to forbear and forgive personally precisely because vengeance belongs to God and He will repay.

study.originalLanguages: David's qinah, zeal, for God's house consumes him, verse 9, the very word John's Gospel applies to Jesus cleansing the temple, John 2:17. And his enemies give him rosh, gall, mixed with his food, verse 21, together with vinegar for his thirst, fulfilled precisely at the cross, Matthew 27:34, 48.

typology.type: Zeal for God's house consuming the psalmist, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Applied directly to Jesus cleansing the temple, John 2:17, His disciples remembering this very psalm.
Quoted by Jesus of Himself, 'they hated Me without a cause,' John 15:25.

typology.type: Gall and vinegar offered for food and drink, verse 21
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled literally at the cross, where Jesus was offered wine mixed with gall and, in His final moments, sour wine, Matthew 27:34, 48, John 19:28-30.

typology.type: The imprecation that the enemy's dwelling be deserted, verse 25
typology.fulfillment: Applied by Peter directly to Judas Iscariot's betrayal and fate, Acts 1:20.

study.christ: This is one of the clearest messianic psalms in the entire Psalter, quoted or echoed more often in the New Testament's account of the passion than nearly any other Old Testament text. Jesus' disciples remembered 'zeal for Your house will consume Me' when He cleansed the temple, John 2:17. Jesus quoted verse 4 of Himself, 'they hated Me without a cause,' John 15:25. He fulfilled verse 21 literally on the cross, given wine mixed with gall and, at the end, sour wine, Matthew 27:34, 48, John 19:28-30. And Peter applies the imprecation of verse 25 directly to Judas Iscariot's fate, Acts 1:20. Even the psalm's hardest verses, its plea for God's justice on the unrepentant, are taken up by the New Testament not as an embarrassment to be explained away but as reliable, Spirit-inspired words fulfilled in the events surrounding the cross.
study.god: God is shown as the one who hears the cry of the drowning and the falsely accused, who allows His anointed to suffer reproach for His own sake, and whose coming justice on the unrepentant, prayed for honestly here, is neither denied nor apologized for anywhere in Scripture, but is entrusted entirely to Him rather than seized by human hands.

crossReferences.note: Applies this psalm's zeal for God's house directly to Jesus cleansing the temple.
Fulfill the gall and vinegar offered to Jesus at the cross, verse 21.
Apply this psalm's imprecations respectively to the judgment on unbelieving Israel and to Judas Iscariot.

study.oneStory: David's undeserved suffering for God's sake, his thirst answered with gall and vinegar, and his plea that God's justice fall on the unrepentant, all find their fulfillment in Christ, whose zeal, thirst, and betrayal this psalm precisely anticipates, and whose cross absorbs the very reproach David describes so that all who trust Him might finally, fully be saved.

sermon.title: Pour Out Your Indignation Upon Them
sermon.why: Piper works through Psalm 69 as one of the most extended imprecatory psalms, showing from its New Testament citations, as the words of David and as the words of Jesus, that its curses are not sinful personal vengeance but sober, Spirit-inspired approval of God's coming justice, and an incentive for believers today to forbear and forgive precisely because vengeance belongs to God.

quiz.q: What image describes David's distress in verses 1-2?

options.text: Waters up to his neck, sunk in miry depths with no footing.
options.note: This drowning imagery pictures overwhelming, inescapable crisis.

options.text: A dry and thirsty land with no water at all.
options.note: That specific image belongs to Psalm 63; this psalm's crisis is too much water, not too little.

options.text: A quiet, peaceful garden.
options.note: The imagery is one of overwhelming crisis, not peace.

options.text: A battlefield strewn with weapons.
options.note: The specific image given is drowning in water and mire, not a battlefield.

quiz.q: Why does David say he has become 'a stranger to my brothers,' verse 8?

options.text: Because zeal for God's house has consumed him.
options.note: His devotion to God's honor itself becomes the reason for his estrangement, later applied to Jesus in John 2:17.

options.text: Because he committed a specific crime against his family.
options.note: The text ties his alienation to zeal for God, not personal wrongdoing against his family.

options.text: Because he moved to a foreign country.
options.note: The estrangement described is relational and spiritual, not geographic relocation.

options.text: Because his family disagreed with his choice of career.
options.note: The specific reason given is religious zeal for God's house, not a career dispute.

quiz.q: What did David's enemies give him instead of comfort, in verse 21?

options.text: Gall for food and vinegar for his thirst.
options.note: This detail is fulfilled literally at the cross, Matthew 27:34, 48.

options.text: Bread and fresh water.
options.note: The verse describes cruelty, gall and vinegar, not kindness.

options.text: Silver and gold as a bribe.
options.note: The specific gift named is bitter food and sour drink, not money.

options.text: Fine wine and rich food.
options.note: The verse specifically names gall and vinegar, deliberately bitter and undesirable, not fine provisions.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply this psalm's zeal 'for Your house,' verse 9?

options.text: To Jesus cleansing the temple, His disciples remembering this very verse.
options.note: John 2:17 explicitly cites this psalm in connection with Jesus' action in the temple.

options.text: To the building of Herod's temple by human labor alone.
options.note: The New Testament connects this verse to Jesus' zeal, not merely to the temple's construction.

options.text: The New Testament never references this verse.
options.note: John 2:17 directly quotes this verse in connection with Jesus.

options.text: To the disciples' zeal in building the early church.
options.note: The specific New Testament application is to Jesus Himself, not the disciples' later ministry.

quiz.q: According to Piper's sermon on this psalm, how should Christians understand its imprecations, verses 22-28?

options.text: As sober, Spirit-inspired words of coming judgment entrusted to God, not sinful personal vengeance.
options.note: The New Testament quotes these very verses approvingly, applying them to God's judgment rather than treating them as sin to be corrected.

options.text: As sinful outbursts the New Testament corrects and disavows.
options.note: The New Testament instead quotes these verses approvingly, in Romans 11 and Acts 1, rather than disavowing them.

options.text: As commands for believers to personally carry out against their enemies today.
options.note: The psalm entrusts judgment to God; it does not instruct believers to execute vengeance personally.

options.text: As poetic exaggeration with no real theological meaning.
options.note: The New Testament treats these words as substantive, meaningful prophecy, not mere poetic exaggeration.

questions: David cries out from waters up to his neck, feeling like he is drowning in his circumstances. What situation right now feels overwhelming enough that you need to cry out to God the way David does here?

David's zeal for God's house made him a stranger even to his own family, and this very zeal is later fulfilled in Christ. Has devotion to God ever cost you a relationship or created distance with people close to you, and how do you process that cost?

David was given gall and vinegar instead of comfort, a detail fulfilled literally at the cross. How does knowing that Christ personally experienced undeserved cruelty change how you bring your own experiences of betrayal or mistreatment to Him?

The psalm's imprecations pray for God's justice rather than seizing revenge personally. Where do you need to hand over a desire for vengeance to God rather than either suppressing it or acting on it yourself?

The psalm ends with hope that heaven, earth, and Zion's rebuilt cities would praise God, verses 34-36. What present difficulty do you need to hold alongside this larger hope, that God's ultimate purpose is restoration and praise, not despair?

translationNotes.note: Those who hate me without cause is quoted by Jesus directly of Himself in John 15:25, applying David's undeserved hostility to His own experience.
Zeal renders qinah, consuming, jealous devotion for God's honor, the very word John's Gospel applies to Jesus cleansing the temple, John 2:17.
May their table become a snare is quoted by Paul in Romans 11:9-10 concerning God's judgment on unbelieving Israel in his own day.
May their place be deserted; let there be no one to dwell in their tents is applied by Peter directly to Judas Iscariot's betrayal and fate in Acts 1:20.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via bible.com (YouVersion, Berean Standard Bible, version 3034) after the local makor-scripture toolkit failed with a persistent disk I/O error reading the sandbox-mounted database. Verse numbering and count (36 verses) verified against Psalm 69:1-69:36 per PSALMS-SECTION-MAP.md. The musical superscription is not rendered as verse text, consistent with the established convention in this corpus. One em dash in the raw fetched source (verse 4) was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch, reading the full text of John Piper's message 'Pour Out Your Indignation Upon Them' at desiringgod.org, which works through this exact psalm's imprecatory theology as honest, Spirit-inspired prayer entrusting justice to God.
